identity.title: Computer and Computer Peripheral Equipment and Software Merchant Wholesalers

lens.included_activities: Independent value-added resellers and IT distributors that repeatedly supply external customers with technology procurement plus managed services, configuration, deployment, lifecycle support, cloud, cybersecurity, or related accountable services, within the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure product box-movers without a recurring or repeat outsourced service, manufacturers and captive sales branches, commission-only agents, software publishers, shells, internal IT units, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, public-sector, and institutional customers buy hardware and software at product margins and may also buy project, maintenance, managed-service, cloud, security, and lifecycle work through recurring contracts or repeat statements of work.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS category mixes product-only distribution with value-added solution providers. The lens is narrowed to firms with a material repeat or recurring outsourced service component so product resale and accountable service delivery are not treated as one business model.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for the broader 423400 industry, not wage-weighted task exposure in 423430. I map the observed concentration in technical sales, software development, customer service, user support, management, and sales management to task-level AI evidence from OpenAI and observed Claude usage; physical fulfillment and field implementation are treated as much less exposed. The broader-industry mix may understate 423430's software and support work, while general AI studies may overstate substitutability because exposure includes augmentation.
a.rationale: The broader 423400 occupation mix is dominated by information-rich commercial and technical roles: 81,780 technical sales representatives, 51,430 other sales representatives, 33,430 software developers, 32,240 customer-service representatives, 29,080 general managers, and 23,770 user-support specialists are among the largest occupations. These roles contain substantial proposal drafting, product matching, quoting, documentation, ticket triage, analysis, and code work, but relationship selling, physical logistics, installation, and accountable design remain human-intensive.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted O*NET task census for a representative sample of 423430 value-added resellers, paired with time studies separating already-automated work from current manual work.
caveats: BLS publishes the relevant occupation list at 423400 rather than 423430.
AI task studies cover the general workforce or Claude users and measure exposure or use, not feasible labor removal.
The screened service-rich firms likely have a different mix from product-only wholesalers.

rho.rationale: Within five years, copilots and workflow agents should be deployable in quoting, CRM updates, proposal generation, renewal preparation, service-desk triage, knowledge retrieval, purchasing, and internal software work. Implementation is capped by fragmented distributor systems, vendor rules, customer security requirements, error costs in configurations and licenses, and the need for sales and technical accountability. Anthropic's observed usage also leans toward augmentation rather than full automation.
rho.change_evidence: Multi-site before-and-after studies from IT resellers reporting workflow-level adoption, exception rates, hours removed, customer defects, and sustained headcount avoidance over at least 12 months.
caveats: No representative five-year implementation study for 423430 was found.
Large public reseller capabilities may overstate implementation capacity at LMM firms.
Benefits depend on data quality and integration with ERP, PSA, vendor, and customer systems.

e.rationale: The default population requires recurring or repeat outsourced service, while the NAICS definition is merchant wholesale. Industry evidence shows a meaningful value-added-reseller segment offering implementation, maintenance, disposal, cloud, cybersecurity, and managed services, but many firms remain primarily product resellers. Eligibility is therefore a minority share of the frozen LMM-band firm count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 423430 LMM companies using websites, service revenue disclosures, and customer contracts to classify material recurring or repeat outsourced-service revenue.
caveats: The frozen n is margin-bridged from product-distribution economics and may not match service-rich reseller economics.
Public-company service breadth is not representative of every LMM wholesaler.
Service revenue is often bundled with product and not separately disclosed.

s5.bridge: Census sources describe owner age across U.S. employer businesses and show that owners aged 55 or older are prominent in wholesale trade, but do not observe five-year control transfers for eligible 423430 firms. I translate the older-owner pool into a five-year qualifying-transfer probability after excluding owners who retain, close, gift internally, or sell nontransferable operations. This can overstate transfers because age is not an exit event and understate sponsor or strategic sales by younger owners.
s5.rationale: Census reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were 55 or older, and its industry analysis identifies wholesale trade as an older-owner sector. A five-year transfer probability well below that stock is appropriate because only some owners transact in any five-year window and only qualifying control sales of transferable operations count.
s5.change_evidence: Firm-level closed-deal and ownership-transition records for 423430 from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and broker interviews, matched to the eligible LMM service-rich population and including non-sale closures.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is broad and partly demographic-subgroup specific.
No denominator-complete 423430 transfer dataset was found.
Strategic roll-ups and succession dynamics may differ for service-rich VARs.

q.rationale: An operator can retain more benefit in fixed-price managed services, bundled support, and internal overhead than in transparent product resale. Retention is limited by intense price competition, vendor-controlled pricing and enterprise-agreement economics, customer procurement scrutiny, renewals, and competitors using productivity gains to reduce fees. PC Connection's 2025 filing reports significant price competition and a total gross margin of 18.8%, while showing materially higher gross margin in its SMB segment, consistent with value capture varying by mix.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level cohort analysis for LMM VARs showing gross-profit retention after automation by revenue type, renewal repricing, win/loss behavior, vendor rebates, and customer pass-through over five years.
caveats: A large public reseller's margin structure is only directional for LMM firms.
Gross margin is not the same as retention of an implemented AI benefit.
Product, project, agency, and managed-service contracts reprice differently.

d5.rationale: Customers should continue replacing and securing endpoints, networks, servers, storage, and cloud estates, while AI infrastructure and cybersecurity create new complexity. PC Connection reported 2025 sales growth and is expanding cloud, AI, automation, and security services, but hardware categories are cyclical, cloud can displace on-premise products, manufacturers can sell direct, and constant-price demand could lag nominal IT spending. The range therefore allows mild contraction through substantial real expansion.
d5.change_evidence: A 423430-specific constant-price five-year forecast separating hardware units, software agency transactions, cloud resale, managed services, and implementation work, validated against distributor backlog and customer renewal cohorts.
caveats: Company growth is not an industry demand measure and includes price, share, and mix effects.
No clean constant-quality volume index was found at six-digit NAICS.
Vendor channel shifts can change distributor demand independently of end-user IT demand.

o.rationale: Self-service portals and vendor-direct cloud purchasing can eliminate some order-taking, catalog navigation, and simple support. Most eligible firms, however, are selected precisely because customers repeatedly outsource solution design, procurement coordination, deployment, security, license governance, maintenance, and lifecycle accountability. PC Connection describes complex infrastructure needs and a service portfolio spanning assessment through disposal, supporting continued operator involvement even as the task mix changes.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level channel migration data showing the share of current VAR service quantities moved to vendor-direct, marketplace, self-service, or software-only delivery, plus interviews with SMB IT buyers on accountable-provider requirements.
caveats: The eligible lens mechanically emphasizes operator-required services.
AI-native vendors and marketplaces may compress the channel faster than historical experience suggests.
Operator requirement varies sharply between commodity procurement and regulated or complex deployments.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is not generic computer distribution but service-rich value-added resellers whose recurring customer relationships combine procurement with managed, cloud, security, implementation, and lifecycle work. Their information-heavy workflows offer substantial AI leverage, although low product margins and vendor-direct channels constrain commercial retention.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can automate first drafts of quotes and proposals, product and license matching, CRM capture, renewal preparation, ticket classification, knowledge retrieval, purchasing exceptions, documentation, and some software or configuration work. Humans remain important for complex architecture, vendor escalation, relationship selling, physical deployment, cybersecurity judgment, and accountability for failures.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price managed services and internal overhead provide the strongest retention paths. Commodity product quotes and renewals are more likely to pass savings to customers through tenders, benchmarks, and channel competition, especially where vendors control terms or transact directly.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen firm count includes many product-centric wholesalers that do not meet the service lens. Among eligible VARs, broad evidence of older wholesale owners supports succession supply, but there is no denominator-complete record of qualifying control transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: Technology refresh, cybersecurity, cloud governance, and AI infrastructure support the service basket, while direct vendor sales, cloud substitution, and self-service threaten commodity intermediation. Durable demand is strongest where the operator integrates multiple vendors and accepts implementation or support accountability.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the eligible share hidden inside a product-wholesale code, the gap between task exposure and actual labor removal, and how quickly competition passes productivity into price. Vendor concentration, rebates, working capital, cyber liability, and customer churn can overwhelm workflow savings.
narrative.principal_driver: A dense mix of technical sales, support, software, and customer-service work creates repeatable AI-enabled workflow capacity in service-rich VARs.
narrative.principal_weakness: Commodity economics and vendor-direct or self-service channels can pass savings away and erode the need for the intermediary.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Observed largest occupations and employment counts for NAICS 423400, including technical and nontechnical sales, software development, customer service, management, user support, repair, and logistics roles.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An early look at the labor market impact potential of large language models
sources.supports: General task-exposure benchmark: about 80% of U.S. workers could have at least 10% of tasks affected and about 19% could have at least 50% affected.

sources.title: The Anthropic Economic Index
sources.supports: Observed Claude task-use patterns: roughly 36% of occupations showed use in at least a quarter of tasks; usage was 57% augmentation and 43% automation, with explicit limitations on representativeness.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: 2019 Annual Business Survey graphic showing 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, 43% were 35-54, and 6% were 34 or younger.

sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age
sources.supports: Census working paper showing older employer owners are prominent in capital-intensive sectors including wholesale trade and reporting wholesale age patterns across demographic groups.

sources.title: PC Connection, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Industry business model, service portfolio, channel competition, customer mix, 2025 sales, gross margins, vendor-controlled enterprise agreements, and investments in cloud, security, AI, and automation services.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 423430 Computer and Computer Peripheral Equipment and Software Merchant Wholesalers
sources.supports: Official industry scope used to distinguish merchant wholesale activity from the narrower service-rich screened lens.
